speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two with interview with Ania Ruddick, nee Kosturek, February 25, 1997. We were talking about when you were growing up in [? Vogovietz. ?] And now I want to ask you if you remember hearing anything about the oncoming war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never had any idea about the war. War to me was just a strange word in the-- on the paper. And I was, at the-- at the time of the first of September, the day of the invasion of Poland by Germany, I was on-- in the pasture watching the cows and having rather melancholy, v lonely stay away from all the kids-- other kids. I liked to stay alone. And all of a sudden, I hear a terrible rumbling, and a plane landed right smack on our-- in our pasture. And I was terribly scared, because I didn't know what he's [INAUDIBLE]. He's going to run me over or something. [LAUGHS] And when he landed-- I have never seen, first, a plane before-- when he landed, I was as amazed as the people in the bush would be, probably amazed at seeing a plane somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the man got out, and he was asking me, where am I? And I told him the name of the place-- of course, trembling all over, being so afraid. And he said, you go call the boys over there, have to ask them for something. So the boys, of course, they were already running to the plane. And he asked them to go to the village and bring some food. And I don't know what else, what other orders he gave them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this man was telling me-- this is war, we are at war with Germans. So you better watch out-- maybe you take your cows home. And the terror stuck everybody there-- they-- he took the cows home. And of course, the whole-- whole [? world-- ?] the whole racket started soon. And I didn't know much about war and what it-- what it meant, really. Except I know-- I knew there was something terrible going to-- something terrible was going to happen. At the moment, I stood there alone-- aside from others-- and I felt almost like a premonition that a chapter of my life is finished and another is going to start-- something is going to happen, something terrible. And it did, of course, very shortly. But before that, before that I was-- I was sent to-- I was really volunteering to go to [? Chevarisk. ?] [? Chevarisk ?] is a city-- not too big a city, but a city where they make a lot of sugar from the beets-- sugar beets. And apparently, the sugar factory, or a sugar place, was bombard-- bombarded. And all the sugar went out in the open. And people were running from all sides to pick up this sugar in the bags-- as much as they could.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I volunteered to go there with my-- my friend, Helen. And we brought, believe me, about 20 pounds of sugar each, and braving-- braving the shooting and so on. Because it was quite a distance-- it was like here to [? Kitchener ?] to walk. And we slept in a-- in a barn over the night. And finally, when we came home with the sugar, we were really heroines-- we were somebody. [LAUGHS] And that was one of the pre-war incidents. But, yes. It's [INAUDIBLE]. How did things change when Germany invaded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things have changed in that way-- first of all, there were changes in my family. Both of my aunts got married. My uncle was married, as well, just a year before the war, had his wife paralyzed. My uncle, a new uncle that came into the house, took over. He put my grandmother, who was the matriarch, he just pushed her aside, and he treated us worse than dogs. And we had to, for example-- one of the examples, we were, of course, most of the time walking barefoot. And it was already November-- very-- quite cold at the time. Some ice was there on the river. And there was this big tree, for example, that fell into the water after the storm. And he-- he was asking us to go into that water and help him to cut the branches. And we did that barefoot and in frigid water. And when we couldn't take it anymore, he asked us to go up on the landing and run until our feet-- feet would regain their heat. And we're going back and forth like that all day, [INAUDIBLE]. One of the examples-- and he just couldn't wait to send us to Germany, when he heard that people are going to be taken to Germany. And of course I, myself, I thought, if I go to Germany, that's not too far to go to France. I'll go to France. I'll find my parents, and everything will be fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of specific people who were being sent away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the specific people. I was-- mostly-- most people were-- there were 10 people chosen from our community. I know about three people I remember. And-- but Helen was the most-- she came with me, I believe. And I had no idea, no idea whatsoever, why we were sort of-- chosen to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- before you were chosen to go, did you know of anything else-- in terms of the little village near you, where there were Jews-- did you know about anything happening to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, before I went, I believe I must have told you, in Sieniawa, they was-- they were forming a ghetto. And we were working, as a matter of fact, in the fields with my brother and my aunts. And out there, I see it was a very-- just rather muddy day-- it was raining at that time. It was in November. And they-- there I see this procession of people being escorted by this gestapo or whatever-- I don't know. At that time, I didn't know what-- what unit he belonged to, or whatever it was. And I was amazed to see-- about, I don't know, there was about 200 people or more-- carrying all kinds of things, and packages, and suitcases, and so on, and-- and tiredly walking. And I don't know where they came from. And they walked into the River San-- because in order to get to Sieniawa, where the ghetto was-- River San-- they had to cross the River San. But the River San didn't have any bridge-- there was only one man who had a small boat, and was taking people one by one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was intrigued seeing those people, walking, painfully, terribly, terribly distressed. And my aunt said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which meant, oh, those people are Jewish. And I say, why? Why should he-- what-- what's that? And she couldn't explain, she just waved her hand-- ha. And all of a sudden, the urge took me to follow them-- and my brother, as well, it was our escape, as well, from-- from our work. And we followed them all the way to the river. They came to the river, and they were so tired. They sat there-- because where would you go? And the men-- the-- the gestapo, or the kapos, I don't know, told them to wait for awhile-- I don't know. They were-- they were eating or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was this lady with this baby-- in her eiderdown-- she was holding this baby so-- like a very precious thing. And some woman-- I believe she was Ukrainian, because I heard by the accent, I understood she was not Polish-- and she said, of course I should not say that in Polish, but she what she said that-- give me that. She wanted the eiderdown. And the woman said, no, I cannot do that, I have a baby in there. And that woman tore this eiderdown from her, and the baby almost fell to the ground. And the lady was-- she grabbed the child just in time, and she was shrieking and crying. And the German gestapo, the German, came towards her, and pushed her towards the river, and said, go, go swim. And then he ordered everybody to go right through the way-- right through the river, when the water was very, very cold. And some people were up to here, and they holding-- they were holding their belongings above their heads. And some were falling, and one was grabbing another in order to not to drown. It was a very shocking sight. And I was standing there, and I was crying, and I couldn't take it anymore. And I remember running. And I was running, and running, and running away from that sight. And I got in one place and sat under a tree, and I was crying there, because I couldn't understand, and I couldn't take it anymore. And my-- my brother, of course, he, being a man, he came and was laughing at me, jeering, and saying, you're a-- coward. You're a [POLISH]. You're a-- [POLISH] is a very mean word in a Polish course, which means old hag, something like that. And-- or a woman that has no-- courage.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to-- to the place, to-- back to my place. And that, of course, that way, little by little, things were changing. And we came, finally, to the front-- to moment where I was chosen for-- to go to Germany. Why I was chosen, you would ask? Because there were some people, somewhere, somebody, hid Chaya-- I mentioned Chaya-- hid Chaya somewhere, and they wanted Chaya right away, because they wanted her to go to the ghetto, and she never.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, either you give us Chaya or 10 of you will go to Germany, to the camps. I had-- had no idea about that, but things in a little village like that go very quietly, secretly. The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bailiff just chose certain people. And since my brother and I, we were indispensible-- we were dispensable, rather-- we were not necessary. We were chosen probably for other people. And, of course, the domestic of Chaya had to send her own daughter. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were taken to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the train. It was a part cattle train, part regular train. However, the regular train was so destroyed-- practically no windows, and then-- and everything is-- well, it's just probably some-- was in some accident, or something. And anyway, it doesn't matter, but we were driven there from-- we were taken to school first, exactly at that place where I was reciting the poems. We're taken from that school, and driven to the-- to the-- to the station and packed solid-- body to body-- in those cars-- boxcars and taken to Krakow. And I had no idea, had never ridden a train-- was never on the train before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ania, so before we go-- go to Krakow, did you have any idea, or did anybody ever tell you who hid Chaya?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the only thing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Helen--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing that I heard from was from-- from my Helen, from my friend Helen. She said to shortly before even this whole expedition came about, she said that, my [? mamja ?] went away. And she left. But where, she didn't-- she didn't say anything, and I was not asking. She went quite-- quite often she went here and there. She was traveling, because she had a daughter somewhere in the schools, in the high school somewhere. So I had no further interest. And-- and I didn't know that I was the hostage, on whatever you would call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- they probably did. I had no idea. We were not taken from that school-- on-- Helen and I, we were-- we were holding on to-- one to another, and we were really separated from the rest, because they were pushed here and there, and-- and it was a havoc, a complete havoc.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- it was-- was it people from your village, the 10 who were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. In that compartment where we were, people were-- some strangers, I don't know who they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if they were Jews or non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if they were Jews or non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some Jews, because I heard their-- their specific speech. There were some Jews, I-- as a matter of fact there was a couple that was next to us, and they were old-- rather elderly people. And this lady was very refined, a very, very nice lady. And I remember she was asking me how old am-- how old am I. And I told her that I am 15, but I was supposed to 16. I'm not 15. Because the bailiff told me, never mention that you're that old-- they will shoot you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told the little lady, and she just caressed my hair, and she said, well, you just stick-- stick to us, here. And we were sitting next to them, and the next thing I know, my-- my friend, Helen, wanted to go to the washroom. So in that crowd, in that terribly stuffy-- but where do we go to the washroom? And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were having such difficulties.

speaker: interviewee
text: We finally got there, and when we got there, we couldn't go back to the couple anymore-- it was so packed, and there was some-- some problem there. The people wanted to jump through the windows or something, because the-- start-- the-- the Germans started shooting. And we were so scared, so we pushed ourselves against the wall, and we just sat there on the floor. And we didn't see anything-- the night was dark. We didn't know where we were going and what we were doing. We were very scared-- two scared girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Krakow, we were driven-- or rather escorted-- on foot to someplace, I believe there was this-- I have no idea what place was it. Because I've never seen that big of city. [DRINKS] And anyway, there was a huge courtyard, and people were formed in quadrangle on all four sides. And in the middle there were policemen and the dogs-- it was sort of between snow and rain, very, very wet time, somewhere around 12:00, maybe 1:00, past midnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which month?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December. We were very scared and very cold. And we didn't know what to-- what to expect. People were being-- next to us, for example, there was this lady who started shrieking. She got-- she was pregnant--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was pregnant, and she started bleeding-- she had probably, what's it called? Was aborting. And-- this-- this old man came out to-- to ask a gestapo if there could be something done for her. He knew some-- some German. And the German just came towards him, and he slugged him right in the middle, in his stomach, with-- in butt of his gun. The man crumpled on the-- on the sidewalk. And the woman, herself, she fell to the ground, and she was just yelling and crying and bleeding all over. And the two kapos came, and they grabbed her by the-- by the feet, and they dragged her away. And that was our first horror story that we have seen. Later on, there were quite a few of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ania, I asked you the month, but I didn't ask you the year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Year?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was December--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was '41, and we were taken-- first of all, there was the role call. And after that, we were taken upstairs-- about three floors upstairs. It was something like-- I believe it must have been either some university, or-- I have-- I don't know Krakow, at all. And to this day, I wouldn't know unless somebody would show me the picture of the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were packed in there-- doesn't-- didn't matter whether there were men or women, everybody went in those small-- small probably lecture rooms. And the kapos stood with their machine guns on-- on the ready, waiting, watching us. And-- some people-- it was first of all, I forgot to tell you, what-- and probably quite a few of the people who survived this know-- when you are so frozen and cold and it's snowing, and there is a drizzle going on, you have to go to the washroom. And no, you cannot move out. You cannot make one step out of the quadrangle. The-- the dogs are just standing there and waiting for you to do that-- make that move. And we had to go. And where do you go? So I was looking around, and all of a sudden, it dawned upon me that people were doing it all-- all over. And the stench was horrible-- even though it was rain-- the rain dispersed a little bit of it. But when we came into that room, the wet undergarments stank terribly. And you-- you could just choke. People were just gasping for breath-- couldn't manage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people were fainting, and in that fainting pose, they simply fell asleep, finally. Because they were too tired-- and so did I, and so did my friend, Helen.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long-- for how long were you in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there about three days. Because, what did I do after I woke up? I decided that I can't take it anymore. I forgot that, OK, we were separated before getting into that room-- so I was separated by-- with my friend Helen was taken to the other side, because we were trying to help this older lady who was fainting. And the kapo grabbed my friend Helen by the collar and took her to the other side of the triangle-- triangle-- the quadrangle. And I was left alone. So when I was left alone-- alone, I was so traumatized, I decided-- if I could just-- when I looked at this-- the third floor, the ramp, I said, I am going to jump, and finish with it. So I did. When we were taken back into the courtyard in the morning, I tried this. And some older man grabbed me by the-- from the back, and took me-- tried to take me away from-- from the side of the ramp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the gestapo noticed that, and he came and grabbed me. And that's where I was taken to the special investigation place, where I was beaten and-- and investigated-- where I come from, and what I am here for, and what-- what's going on, and this and that. And this man finally looked in his papers, and he-- he started questioning me, where is Chaya? We're gonna take a break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
